text: An AI agent can fail even when its model is good.
Context MRI works like a medical scan for the instructions around that model.
It finds the one file causing damage, measures the effect, and checks that removing it really helps.
Here is the whole test.
Give Context MRI the job, the files the agent reads, and a clear example of success.
It runs the same task several times, changing only one file at a time.
Everything else stays fixed, so the comparison is fair.
In this example, an old API guide disagrees with the current Responses API schema.
With every file included, the agent scores forty-three.
Remove the old guide, and it scores ninety-two in all three repeats,
while using forty-four percent fewer context tokens.
And you do not have to trust a mystery score.
Open any result to see the prompt, the model's answer, the scoring rubric, speed, and token use.
Twenty-one evidence records show the improvement happens again and again,
not just once by luck.
That turns the finding into a clear next step:
remove or rewrite the harmful file, use the smaller verified context pack, and test again.
Even a suggested fix stays untrusted until the scores show that it works.
The public judge demo replays one fixed experiment,
so anyone can explore the full workflow without an account or API key.
It is clearly labeled as a replay.
With funded quota, live mode runs the same controlled test
through GPT-five-point-six Sol and the Responses API.
Why does this matter?
Teams can spend hours blaming the prompt or buying a stronger model
when one bad file is the real problem.
Context MRI replaces that guesswork with evidence,
helping agents become more accurate, less expensive, and easier to trust.
